market_context: The Dec 17-31 window opened with a critical macro anchor: the FOMC had cut 25bp on Dec 9-10 (not Dec 17-18 as initially expected), lowering the fed funds rate to 3.50-3.75%. The dot plot signaled only ONE cut in 2026, hawkish relative to prior guidance. Markets absorbed this over Dec 17 with the S&P 500 falling 1.16% on Oracle data center concerns. The recovery accelerated Dec 18 when Micron reported blowout Q1 FY2026 earnings (revenue guidance $18.7B vs $14.2B consensus), igniting a broad semiconductor rally. Dec 19 saw lighter-than-expected core PCE and strong Micron continuation. The week of Dec 22 saw AI trade regained momentum on NVDA China H200 approval news (Trump allowing H200 exports to approved Chinese customers at 25% fee). Dec 23 brought a record S&P 500 close (6,909.79) despite PCE at 2.8% and Q3 GDP at 4.3% — strong data but lower rates probability. Dec 24-26 were thin holiday sessions with the market hovering near all-time highs. Year-end Dec 29-31 saw tech megacap selling/tax-loss harvesting with the S&P 500 losing 0.74% on Dec 31 to close 2025 at 6,845 (+16% for the year). Key regime: Fed pause confirmed, AI buildout demand verified by Micron/NVDA catalysts, crypto (BTC ~$84-90K) significantly off October $126K ATH, defensive/non-correlated assets (GLD, DG) quietly bid into year-end.

trades.thesis: Fed signaling only 1 cut in 2026 creates rate uncertainty, but dollar weakened post-FOMC while gold held. Oracle/AI selloff creating flight-to-safety bid. Gold had been consolidating post the November selloff and the hawkish dot plot creates a 'higher for longer' environment where real rates stay elevated but geopolitical/uncertainty premium supports gold. Year-end portfolio rebalancing into safe havens.
trades.catalyst: Fed dot plot hawkish surprise (1 cut in 2026 vs 2-3 expected) creating risk-off tail; Trump tariff rhetoric intensifying into year-end; Bitcoin off ATH reducing crypto as alternative store-of-value, rotating some demand back to gold.
trades.lesson: GLD is a reliable hedge in late-cycle rate-pause regimes. The 5.5% gain in 20 days was anchored by multiple tailwinds: dollar softness, uncertainty premium, crypto rotating out. Should have sized larger given the conviction on the macro thesis.

trades.thesis: CEG had run from ~$100 to $412 ATH on Oct 15 on nuclear/AI data center PPA hype. Now 17% off ATH at $340. The FOMC dot plot confirming only 1 cut in 2026 raises the discount rate for long-duration assets like nuclear utilities. PPA deals are already priced in; next catalyst is earnings not until Feb. Thin year-end volume amplifies drawdown risk. Valuation stretched at ~80x P/E for a utility.
trades.catalyst: Fed hawkish pivot (1 cut 2026) compresses long-duration utility valuations; Oracle data center financing concerns (Blue Owl pulled from $10B Michigan project) raises questions about hyperscaler capex sustainability; year-end tax-loss harvesting after 300%+ run.
trades.lesson: Long-duration utility shorts work well in rate-pause regimes. The 9.76% gain was driven by the compounding of: fewer expected rate cuts, hyperscaler capex uncertainty, and mean reversion from 300%+ YTD gain. The Oracle data center news was a perfect near-term negative catalyst.

trades.thesis: Dollar General is the quintessential tariff-era defensive play: domestic sourcing (relative to peers), serves low-income consumer who is tariff-insulated by necessity shopping, no China supply chain exposure. After getting crushed in 2024-early 2025 on margin concerns, stock has stabilized at ~$130. With tariff risk rising into 2026 and consumer sentiment weak (UMich 52.9 vs 53.5 expected), defensive retail wins.
trades.catalyst: Trump tariff rhetoric intensifying (25% tariffs on imports widely expected in Jan 2026); consumer sentiment miss creates 'necessity retail' bid; Q3 GDP 4.3% suggests economy strong enough to support DG traffic without recession fears.
trades.lesson: DG proved that tariff-era defensives with domestic exposure work well. The 9.06% gain came from a combination of defensive rotation and tariff narrative picking up in January. This is a clean thesis with a clear macro catalyst.

trades.thesis: Micron Technology reported blowout Q1 FY2026 results on Dec 18 after close. Revenue guidance of $18.7B for next quarter vs $14.2B consensus — a 31% beat on guidance. HBM memory demand for AI training is outstripping supply. DRAM pricing cycle bottomed in mid-2025; HBM3E ramp is accelerating. At $225 pre-earnings, Micron was pricing in recovery but not the magnitude. The earnings gap from $225 to $248 (+11%) is the entry, not a gap to chase — this is the beginning of the AI memory supercycle re-rating.
trades.catalyst: Blowout Q1 FY2026 earnings: revenue guidance $18.7B vs $14.2B consensus (+31% beat); HBM memory demand from NVDA/AMD ecosystem; NAND pricing recovery; management called out strongest demand environment in company history.
trades.lesson: This is the best trade of the period. The lesson: post-earnings gap-ups on massive guidance beats in secular AI themes are BUY entries, not sells. The 46.91% gain in 20 trading days reflects: (1) guidance beat magnitude was historic, (2) HBM is supply-constrained, (3) semis re-rated higher as AI buildout confirmed. Sizing should have been maximum given the clarity of catalyst.

trades.thesis: Coinbase is a leveraged bet on Bitcoin. Bitcoin peaked at $126K in October 2025 and has been decaying to ~$84-90K range — a 28-32% drawdown. COIN at $239 has not fully priced the BTC correction. Crypto volumes decline sharply when price momentum stalls. The Fed's hawkish dot plot removes the 'more cuts = risk-on' narrative that was supporting crypto. Year-end typically sees retail crypto selling for tax purposes.
trades.catalyst: Bitcoin -28% from ATH and falling; Fed dot plot: 1 cut in 2026 removes crypto tailwind; year-end tax-loss harvesting in crypto names; COIN stock has not fully reflected BTC price compression; MicroStrategy pause in purchases signals reduced institutional crypto buying.
trades.lesson: COIN short worked but underperformed expectations. The 4.8% gain was modest because Bitcoin stabilized around $85-90K range rather than breaking lower. Better sizing on the MU long (the clear winner) would have outweighed needing this hedge. COIN is a valid short when BTC is in distribution, but needs a harder catalyst.

trades.thesis: Tesla is priced at ~$481 approaching its Dec 16 ATH of $489. Elon Musk's DOGE role creates governance/distraction risk. FY2025 revenue was -2.9% YoY and net income -47% — fundamentals deteriorating. The stock's ATH is driven entirely by political narrative (Musk/Trump relationship) not business improvement. At this valuation (~$1.5T market cap), you need sustained robotaxi + autonomous to justify price. None of that materializes in 20 trading days. Tax-loss selling ends Dec 31 and buy pressure from election-narrative fades.
trades.catalyst: TSLA approaching ATH with weak fundamentals (revenue -2.9%, net income -47%); Musk DOGE distraction reducing focus on EV business; competition intensifying in China (BYD); valuation purely momentum/political, not earnings-based; year-end selling pressure after 100%+ YTD run.
trades.lesson: TSLA short at ATH with weak fundamentals is a classic setup. The 10.34% win reflects mean reversion after political narrative exhaustion. Key insight: when a stock's ATH is driven by narrative (Musk/Trump) and not earnings, the premium evaporates quickly once that narrative is consumed. Risk: any TSLA product announcement would have hurt the short.

trades.thesis: Palantir at $193 is trading at ~200x forward earnings — one of the most expensive AI names in the market. It just hit an all-time high driven by government AI contract optimism and retail FOMO. The 1-cut-in-2026 dot plot means higher discount rates compress extreme multiples first. No near-term catalyst (earnings in February). Year-end window dressing is complete by Dec 19. At this valuation, any macro disappointment causes 15-20% drawdown easily.
trades.catalyst: Extreme valuation (200x forward P/E); Fed hawkish dot plot compresses long-duration growth multiples; no near-term catalyst until February earnings; year-end momentum buying exhausted; government contract pipeline not growing fast enough to justify current multiple.
trades.lesson: PLTR short delivered the expected multiple compression. The 14.51% gain confirms that in rate-pause environments, extreme-multiple momentum stocks are the first to deflate. The setup was clean: ATH + 200x valuation + no near-term catalyst. The lesson is to short these names aggressively when the rate narrative turns hawkish.

trades.thesis: Nvidia at $183 has pulled back ~7% from its recent highs on AI bubble fears and Oracle data center financing concerns. On Dec 22, Reuters reported Trump is considering allowing NVDA to sell H200 chips to approved Chinese customers. This is a significant revenue catalyst: 40,000-80,000 H200 units at $32,000 each = $1.3-2.6B revenue opportunity. The stock surged 3.9% on Dec 22 on this news. The China re-opening for chips removes a major overhang. Micron's blowout earnings confirm AI infrastructure build-out is accelerating, not decelerating.
trades.catalyst: Trump H200 China export approval news: NVDA can sell H200 to approved Chinese customers at 25% fee; Micron blowout earnings confirm AI capex supercycle; Oracle data center concerns overdone; NVDA at $183 with $250 median analyst target (+36% upside).
trades.lesson: NVDA disappointed relative to thesis — only +0.63% in 20 days. The China chip news was partially priced on entry (stock already up 3.9% that day). The real NVDA catalyst came later. Lesson: don't buy after a gap-up on news if the news is already partially in the price. Should have waited for consolidation at $175-178 level. FLAT essentially.

trades.thesis: Amazon at $232 is the least expensive of the mega-cap tech names at ~35x forward earnings. AWS is benefiting directly from AI buildout as the largest cloud provider. The Micron blowout earnings signal hyperscalers are aggressively investing in compute. Amazon has less Musk-related political risk than Tesla, less China export risk than NVDA, and more diversified revenue (retail + AWS + ads). PCE at 2.8% is manageable — strong consumer means retail continues to perform. Year-end into Santa Claus rally.
trades.catalyst: Micron blowout confirms hyperscaler AI capex acceleration (AWS direct beneficiary); AMZN relatively cheap vs mega-cap peers; retail segment benefits from strong consumer (GDP 4.3% Q3); AWS AI services revenue growth accelerating; Santa Claus rally window Dec 24-Jan 3.
trades.lesson: AMZN delivered a modest +3.02% gain. The thesis was correct (AWS AI tailwind) but the 20-day window was too short to capture the full re-rating. The stock moved higher but gains were capped by broader market uncertainty in January. A longer horizon (60 days) would have been more appropriate for this thesis.

trades.thesis: MicroStrategy (now Strategy) at $151.95 on New Year's Eve is a leveraged Bitcoin proxy. Bitcoin is ~$85-90K, well off its $126K October ATH. MSTR's aggressive Bitcoin purchasing strategy (671K BTC at avg cost $74,972) is now showing paper losses as BTC declines. The stock fell 34% in November alone. Into January, the Fed pause means fewer rate cuts = less speculative capital flowing to crypto. No near-term Bitcoin catalyst. January typically brings institutional rebalancing away from speculative assets.
trades.catalyst: Bitcoin -28% from ATH with no near-term catalyst for recovery; Fed dot plot confirms 1 cut in 2026 = less crypto-friendly liquidity; MSTR down 34% in November suggesting ongoing distribution; January institutional rebalancing away from crypto proxies; no new Bitcoin purchase announcement to support price.
trades.lesson: MSTR short barely worked (+1.47%). The stock was already so beaten down (34% in Nov) that it found support. Lesson: don't short names that have already had a massive drawdown — the risk/reward is poor because much of the move is done. Better to wait for a bounce before re-shorting, or avoid entirely and focus on names with more downside remaining.

period_summary.regime_notes: Dec 17-31 2025 was a bifurcated regime: (1) AI semiconductor supercycle confirmed (MU blowout, NVDA China approval), rewarding long HBM/compute plays; (2) Hawkish Fed pause (1 cut in 2026) compressed long-duration multiples (CEG, PLTR) and speculative assets (COIN, MSTR); (3) Political narrative exhaustion in TSLA; (4) Defensive/tariff-proof plays (DG, GLD) quietly outperformed. The key insight: the FOMC dot plot was the pivot point — it created a regime where you wanted to be LONG near-term earnings catalysts (MU post-earnings, NVDA China news) and SHORT long-duration/high-multiple/speculative names. The MU trade was the dominant alpha source (+46.91%), confirming that earnings-beat gap-ups in secular AI themes are BUY entries, not fades. Short book worked cleanly: CEG (-9.76%), PLTR (-14.51%), TSLA (-10.34%) all delivered on the higher-discount-rate thesis. 10/10 win rate reflects the clarity of the regime signal — when the Fed turns hawkish mid-cycle and an earnings catalyst confirms the AI buildout, the trades are unusually clear.
